The idea here is the better prompting is usually less about magic wording and more about really giving clear instructions to ChatGPT and as much context and a clear picture of the outcome that you want. Instant is what we'll default to. It's what we've been using today. When the task is more complex or you want the model to reason more carefully, Thinking is usually the better fit. And then for the most advanced or high-stakes work, Pro can give you the strongest reasoning. So the simple rule of thumb here, start with Instant, move to Thinking for more complex work, and then use Pro whenever the depth matters most. If I wanted a full list of the sources, I can also open them by clicking this Sources and Activity button here in the top right. But these are the citations, and if at any point you want to review any of the citations, they're not only in line, but also listed here. And then what's awesome is I can also see its line of thinking. So if I click into Activity, this button up here on the right, I can look at the specific thought process that it went through as it researched. Really quick feature I'll show here, that I do love to show is that if at any point in your chat you just want to go back and take the conversation in a different direction. So let's find an example. So let's say we go back to this example where I was talking about marine sciences deep research reports, and instead of going into microbial and viral control of climate-driven ecosystem shifts, I instead wanted to go into a different topic. I could click these three buttons at the bottom of the chat before it, and I can branch this in a new chat.What this is going to do is open up that chat. Everything before it will also be included and all that context, and it's given me a branch. So now I can ask for a deep research report on a separate topic and keep the first thread on the microbial-specific topic. So just an interesting way to be able to branch your chats into a new chat in the case that you want to take a chat in two different directions. Awesome. So last part of today before we open up for Q&amp;A, most of what we've looked at today has been conversational AI, right? You ask ChatGPT for something, it responds, and then you guide the conversation from there. Agents are a newer concept and are a little bit different. So they can take on a broader workflow with more independence. So you give them a goal and context and instructions and access to the right tools, and then an agent can work through multiple steps with less manual steering. So that shift is important in higher education because a lot of the work that you're doing tends to be repeatable. So we're still early here on agents, but they're going to become a big part of how folks are using AI, especially in universities where the work is complex and contextual and ongoing. OpenAI has two kind of main agent offerings right now. We have Workspace Agents, which I'm going to demo in just a moment, and that lives inside ChatGPT. And then we have a separate app, which is called Codex, that's really great. It started as more of a coding tool, but it's becoming really relevant for knowledge workers and for non-technical folks as well. We're not going to spend time today on Codex, but I would encourage you, if you're interested in that product, to go look into it. It's really kind of the future of where I think we're going as an industry and in the way that folks interact with AI. Workspace Agents is an amazing way that we've taken a lot of the great parts of Codex and brought it into the ChatGPT workspace. So we'll talk through what that looks like. Workspace Agents, right? For faculty, a huge value is just time back, and agents is a way to help with just repetitive computer work that sits around teaching or research or administration. Think of them almost like AI coworkers that can help move work forward across the tools that your team already uses. They can research, summarize, build updates. They can monitor progress without you having to actually jump between the systems or guide it. You just tell it once. So that means less time chasing details and more time focused on, I think, the work that is more interesting, which is working with students and this gives just a really nice way to cut out some administrative time. Because they are built for organizations, these can be deployed at a university with the right permissions and visibility and guardrails. I'll show you what these Workspace Agents look like in chat. They're pretty new, so there's a chance you're not seeing them yet in your workspace if your administrator has not turned them on. But I would ask for them in the case that you're not seeing them because they're super powerful. So in this example, we'll open up this sidebar, and we're going to go to this Agents section here in ChatGPT. So this is on the left-hand side, we have Agents. We're going to click Create Agent. So this is what the agent builder looks like, and it's really conversational. That's the whole point. It's not meant to be overly complicated. You just can start by describing what you want to do. So here I'm going to say, "Create a Workspace Agent called the Course Operations Agent. It should help Dr. Maya Chen, who's a professor of biology, just manage weekly operations for her class." It's a sociology class. "This agent should help with drafting a weekly course brief, summarizing questions and themes that are coming from my students, comparing the upcoming week's plan against the syllabus, suggesting reminders, things like that. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to send this, and ChatGPT is going to start to think as it takes in this context. This might take a little bit of time, but I'll quickly frame up the agent. It's really meant to help this specific faculty member manage just the recurring operational work around her course. So things like drafting weekly course briefs and summarize student questions and checking whether the week's plan matches the syllabus, helping me as an instructor just stay organized. So you're going to see that chat will output a suggestion for how we go. So this is the plan for the agent. It hasn't executed on it yet, but this is what it's thinking of doing. It's summarized the point of this agent. I've given it some channels. These are different things that it's allowed to do. You can add on this. If you have Slack, you could say you're allowed to respond in Slack, or if you want to connect it to Gmail, you can say, "You can help me draft responses to students in Gmail." I want you to write the first draft to any student questions that come in, and I want you to write them in this tone of voice. Here are some samples of how I like to write.
